sound_type: squeak + high whistle -- male: thin high whistle (weee-ooo, 3,000-6,000 Hz); female: harsh ek-ek-ek (1,000-3,000 Hz). Not a quack. Mouse-like. E=4. Audible in turbulent water by frequency placement above noise.
ecosystem: Turbulent stream (breeding) + rocky coastline (winter). Breeds on fast-flowing mountain streams in Cascades and Olympics. Winters on exposed rocky coast. Turbulence at every stage. Mountain torrent or ocean breaker -- the Harlequin seeks chaos.
season: Year-round PNW presence. Breeds on mountain streams spring/summer. Migrates to rocky coast for winter. Dual-habitat lifecycle connecting freshwater and marine ecosystems.
family: Anatidae (FIRST ENTRY -- NEW FAMILY in the engine)

coordinates.description: Reference for turbulent mountain stream breeding habitat in the Cascades. Same stream habitat as American Dipper (degrees 116-117). Winter: shifts to rocky coastline (Olympic coast, ~47.9N, -124.6W).

taxonomy_note.description: FIRST ANATIDAE ENTRY -- NEW FAMILY in the engine. The duck/goose/swan family enters. ~174 species globally. One of the most ecologically important bird families in the PNW. Sole species in genus Histrionicus (monotypic). Named after Harlequin of commedia dell'arte. Histrionicus = actor (Latin histrio).
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Anatidae count: 1 entry (Harlequin Duck 118). NEW FAMILY.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Histrionicus from Latin histrio (actor, stage player). The scientific name means 'actor actor' -- the most theatrical bird in North American ornithology. Common name Harlequin from the costumed commedia dell'arte character Arlecchino, referencing the bird's elaborately patterned plumage.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Histrionicus -- monotypic genus containing only the Harlequin Duck. Taxonomically isolated. No close relatives among sea ducks.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Anseriformes -- the waterfowl order. First Anseriformes entry in the engine. After Passeriformes (most entries), Piciformes (Picidae nonet), and Strigiformes (owls). A major order enters.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Anatidae is new. First entry at degree 118. The duck family opens enormous taxonomic territory for the engine.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Male whistle: 3,000-6,000 Hz (thin, high-pitched). Female alarm: 1,000-3,000 Hz (harsh, lower).
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Male: thin high whistle (weee-ooo, seee), 3,000-6,000 Hz. Female: harsh ek-ek-ek, 1,000-3,000 Hz. Male also: low hoarse croak during aggression.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Calls are brief: 0.3-1.0 seconds. Not continuous like dipper song. Given intermittently during display, alarm, or social interaction.
song_metrics.note_structure: SQUEAK + WHISTLE: Male whistle is thin, high, almost mouse-like. Female call is harsh, staccato. In groups: thin whistles and harsh notes create unusual acoustic texture. Not the quack expected from a duck.
song_metrics.note_types: Male display whistle (primary), female alarm call, male aggressive croak. The whistle is the diagnostic vocalization.
song_metrics.timbre: Thin. High-pitched. Squeaky. The timbre is surprising from a duck -- more delicate than expected. The high pitch cuts through turbulent water noise by occupying frequencies above the noise floor.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=4 -- moderate. Carries over turbulent water but does not dominate the acoustic environment. Audible in chaos through frequency placement, not volume. The Harlequin does not out-shout the water (like the dipper at E=5); it squeaks above it.
song_metrics.context: DISPLAY AND SOCIAL. Male whistle given during courtship display and social interaction. Female call given during alarm and group coordination. The squeaky voice is surprising given the bird's dramatic visual presence.
song_metrics.key_property: SQUEAK + HIGH WHISTLE -- not a quack. FIRST ANATIDAE ENTRY -- NEW FAMILY. The duck family enters at degree 118. Named after Harlequin of commedia dell'arte. Histrionicus = actor. Slate blue plumage with white crescents and chestnut flanks. Breeds on turbulent mountain streams (dipper habitat 116-117). Winters on rocky coastline. Turbulence at every stage. E=4. The painted player.

plumage.overall: Male: Slate blue body with bold white crescents (between eye and bill, behind ear, on neck), chestnut crown stripe and flanks, white spots on flanks and scapulars. Geometric precision -- looks painted on. Female: brownish overall with lighter face patches. Extreme sexual dimorphism.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: For degree 118: (1) SLATE BLUE BODY with white crescents and chestnut flanks (male). (2) SMALL, COMPACT sea duck on turbulent water. (3) WHITE CRESCENT between eye and bill (male, diagnostic). (4) CHESTNUT FLANKS contrasting with slate blue. (5) Female: brownish with two pale face spots.
plumage.seasonal_variation: Male eclipse molt (late summer): dull plumage resembling female. The theatrical costume comes off temporarily. Breeding plumage (fall through spring): full spectacular display.
plumage.flight_pattern: Fast, direct, low over water. Small, compact body in flight. Often in small groups. Wing beats rapid.

physical_description.overall: Small, compact sea duck. Body length 38-45 cm. Wingspan 63-67 cm. Weight 500-680 g. Smaller than most sea ducks. Compact build adapted to turbulent water -- reduced drag in fast current and surf.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 118: Small dark duck on turbulent water (rapids or surf zone). If the water is violent and the duck is small with slate blue plumage and white facial marks -- Harlequin Duck. No other PNW duck routinely occupies such turbulent habitat.
physical_description.field_id: Small, compact duck on turbulent water. Male: slate blue with white crescents and chestnut flanks -- unmistakable if seen clearly. Female: dark brown with pale face spots. Always in turbulent habitat: mountain rapids or coastal surf zone. High-pitched squeak, not a quack.

nodes.title: Anatidae -- NEW FAMILY -- The Duck Family Enters
nodes.description: First Anatidae entry in the engine. Ducks, geese, swans. ~174 species globally. One of the most ecologically important PNW bird families. Major taxonomic event at degree 118. The family enters with the most visually striking species, not the most common.

nodes.title: The Painted Player -- Theatrical Plumage
nodes.description: Slate blue body + white crescents + chestnut flanks. Geometric precision. Each mark looks painted on. Named after Harlequin of commedia dell'arte. Histrionicus histrionicus = 'actor actor.' The most theatrical bird in North American ornithology. Nature as performance.

nodes.title: Turbulent Water Specialist
nodes.description: Breeds on mountain stream rapids. Winters in coastal surf zone. Turbulence at every stage of life cycle. Small, compact body adapted to chaotic water. Where other ducks seek calm, the Harlequin seeks chaos. Dual-habitat: freshwater (breeding) + marine (winter).

nodes.title: Commedia Dell'Arte -- Theater in Taxonomy
nodes.description: Named for Harlequin (Arlecchino): costumed, acrobatic, mercurial trickster of commedia dell'arte (16th century Italy). The intersection of natural history and performance art. Taxonomy recognized the bird's theatrical appearance. Nature imitating art.

nodes.title: Squeak + Whistle -- The Unexpected Voice
nodes.description: Not a quack. High-pitched squeak and thin whistle. Mouse-like. Male: weee-ooo (3,000-6,000 Hz). Female: harsh ek-ek-ek (1,000-3,000 Hz). E=4. Carries above turbulent water noise by frequency placement. Surprising from a duck.

cross_references.relationship: HABITAT OVERLAP. Both breed on turbulent mountain streams. Same prey: caddisfly, stonefly, mayfly larvae. Dipper walks underwater; Harlequin dives from surface. Three consecutive aquatic degrees (116-117-118).
DIVING COMPARISON. Both dive in fast-flowing streams. Dipper: wing hydrofoils, walks bottom. Harlequin: surface dive, swims. Passerine adapted to water vs. duck adapted to turbulence.
WATER CONTINUATION (4 DEGREES). Fire (115) -> stream/song (116) -> stream/dunk (117) -> turbulent stream/duck (118). The aquatic era. Four consecutive ecosystem entries involving water.
FIRST PICIDAE vs FIRST ANATIDAE. Flicker: first Picidae (degree 21). Harlequin: first Anatidae (degree 118). Both family firsts. Introducing woodpeckers vs. introducing ducks.
VISUAL CONTRAST. Spotted Owl: cryptic (brown, camouflaged). Harlequin Duck: spectacular (slate blue, white crescents, chestnut). Maximum visual contrast. The owl hides; the duck displays.
